## Public-key cryptography separates disclosure from control

Symmetric cryptography uses one shared secret. That can work when two parties already
trust each other enough to exchange the key safely, but it becomes awkward in open
networks with many participants.

Public-key cryptography changes the setup. Each participant has a key pair:

- a private key, which must stay secret,
- and a public key, which can be shared.

That split is what makes blockchain authorization practical. A user can publish an
address or public key for receiving funds without revealing the secret needed to
authorize spending.

## What the key pair is doing

The public and private keys are mathematically linked, but not interchangeable.

- The private key is the control object.
- The public key is the verification or receiving object.

The course does not need students to derive elliptic-curve formulas by hand. The
important point is the workflow logic: anyone can know the public key, but only the
holder of the private key should be able to produce valid authorizations.

## Why this matters in cryptocurrency systems

A blockchain ledger cannot rely on "the bank recognizes my identity." It needs a
protocol-level way to check who is allowed to move a coin or approve a transaction.

Public-key cryptography provides that starting point:

- users generate key pairs,
- ownership rules point to public keys or derived addresses,
- and later transactions must provide cryptographic proof tied to the corresponding
  private key.

This gives the system a notion of control without requiring a centralized account
manager.

::: {.teacher-only}
Students often confuse "public key" with "public identity." It is worth stressing
that a public key proves control relations inside the protocol, not civil identity in
the legal sense.
:::

## What a public key does not do

Students should also be clear about the limits.

- A public key does not reveal the private key.
- A public key does not by itself prove who a person is off-chain.
- A public key does not protect message confidentiality unless the system is using it
  for encryption.

In this course, the immediate role is not encryption but authorization.

## What students should be able to explain

After this concept, students should be able to explain:

- how asymmetric cryptography differs from shared-secret cryptography,
- why blockchains need key pairs to separate receiving from spending authority,
- what the private key controls,
- what the public key can safely reveal,
- and why protocol authorization is not the same thing as real-world identity.
